senses.gloss: to separate, to become distinct, to sunder
to be or become evident, to come out, to be or become clearly visible by way of separation
ben tree, horseradish tree (the Moringa oleifera of Arabia and India, which produces oil of ben)

examples.text: بان شجرة معروفة لها ثمرة حبّها أكبر من الحمص مائل إلى البياض طيب الرائحة وله لبّ دهني قال الشيخ الرئيس إنه ينفع من البرص والكلف والبهق وآثار القروح وينفع من الثآليل أيضا في المراهم وطبخه ينفع من وجع الأسنان مضمضة وقال غيره ينفع من الجرب ويقطع الرعاف.
examples.english: The ben is a well-known tree with a fruit the grains of which are larger than chickpeas with a tendency towards white and a nice smell and an oily pulp about which Šayḵ ar-Raʾīs says that it helps against leprosy and melasmata and vitiligo and traces of ulcers and it helps against warts, too, and the salves and decocts applied by bandage are useful against toothache, and another author said it relives scabies and staunches nosebleed.

senses.gloss: ban, a title in the Southeastern Europe

etymology.text:
Derived from the active participle of بَنَى (banā, “to build, to erect, to construct”).

From ب و ن (b w n), as also بَنَان (banān, “finger”), a reference to the fruit or branch shape, known for their elegance in Arabic poetry.

From Ottoman Turkish بان (ban), from Proto-Slavic *banъ.
